hook: You already know how to compute probabilities for simple events — a coin flip, drawing a card, rolling a die. But real decisions rarely look like that. When a doctor tells you a test is '95% accurate,' should you panic? When an airline overbooks a flight, how do they decide how many extra tickets to sell? The gap is turning raw probability numbers into actual decisions, and the question is: how do you weigh uncertainty against real-world costs and consequences?
concept: Probability-based decision making means choosing the action that minimizes risk or maximizes benefit by computing the expected value of each option. In plain English: you multiply each possible outcome's value by its probability, then sum to get a single number you can compare across choices. Formally, the expected value of a random variable $X$ with outcomes $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ and probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$ is $E[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \, p_i$, and the rational decision is the one with the highest (or lowest, if measuring cost) expected value.
anatomy: A probability-based decision problem has four named components: the action set — the list of distinct choices available to the decision-maker; the outcome space — all possible results for each action, each assigned a numerical value (gain, loss, utility, or cost); the probability distribution — the likelihood $p_i$ attached to each outcome, which must satisfy $\sum p_i = 1$ for each action; and the expected value $E[X] = \sum x_i p_i$ — the weighted average payoff that serves as the single comparison number. The decision rule is then to select the action with the optimal expected value.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Action A: carry umbrella. Action B: don't carry umbrella.}
worked_example.description_latex: We first lay out the decision clearly by naming every action available. Here a commuter must decide whether to carry an umbrella given a 40% chance of rain, so we label the two actions before assigning any numbers.

Next we assign numerical values to every outcome. Carrying an umbrella always costs a minor inconvenience of $-1$ (effort), while getting caught without one in the rain costs $-10$ (misery), and staying dry without one costs nothing; assigning numbers forces vague preferences into a form we can compute.

We compute the expected value of Action A by multiplying each payoff by its probability and summing. Every scenario leads to the same $-1$ cost for carrying the umbrella, so the weighted sum is simply $-1$, which is our baseline to beat.

Now we compute the expected value of Action B by the same method. The rain penalty of $-10$ occurs with probability $0.4$, contributing $-4$ to the average, while the dry outcome contributes nothing, giving an expected cost of $-4$.

We compare the two expected values and select the action with the higher (less negative) value. Because $-1 > -4$, carrying the umbrella is the rational choice — it avoids a large expected loss, even though there is a 60% chance the umbrella turns out unnecessary.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{A factory can run inspection (cost }\$200\text{) or skip it.}\\\text{Defect probability} = 0.15.\; \text{Recall cost if defect reaches market} = \$2000.
partial_example.description_latex: We read the problem carefully and identify two actions: inspect or skip. Before computing anything, note that the inspection eliminates recall risk entirely (it catches defects), while skipping leaves a 15% chance of a costly recall — this asymmetry is what makes the expected-value comparison meaningful.

The inspection cost is certain, so its expected value is simply $-200$ with no probability weighting needed. This is your baseline — now you need to find the expected cost of skipping to see which action is cheaper on average.

Compute $E[\text{Skip}]$ by multiplying each outcome's cost by its probability and summing. Remember there are two outcomes: a defect (probability $0.15$, cost $\$2000$) and no defect (probability $0.85$, cost $\$0$). What is $E[\text{Skip}]$?
Compare $E[\text{Inspect}]$ and $E[\text{Skip}]$ and state which action the factory should choose, explaining in one sentence why the expected-value criterion justifies that choice.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A lottery ticket costs \$2. It pays \$10 with probability $0.1$ and nothing otherwise. Compute $E[\text{net gain}]$ for buying the ticket.
You can take Route A (always 30 min) or Route B (20 min with probability $0.6$, 50 min with probability $0.4$). Which route has the lower expected travel time?
A tech company offers two bonuses: Bonus X pays \$5000 with certainty. Bonus Y pays \$12000 with probability $0.35$ and \$0 otherwise. Which has the higher expected value, and by how much?

An insurance policy costs \$150 per year. Without it, there is a $0.03$ probability of a \$6000 loss and a $0.97$ probability of no loss. Using expected value, determine whether buying the insurance is the rational choice and compute the expected savings (or extra cost) from insuring.
practice_problems.answer_latex: E[\text{uninsured loss}]=6000(0.03)=180.\; \text{Policy costs }150<180,\; \text{so buy insurance; saves }E=\$30 \text{ per year.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A physician recommends a treatment that cures a disease (value $+100$ quality-adjusted life days) with probability $0.7$, has no effect with probability $0.2$, and causes a serious side effect (value $-60$ days) with probability $0.1$. The alternative is no treatment (value $0$). Should the patient choose treatment based on expected value?

common_mistakes: Students forget to subtract the cost of an action from the payoffs, computing expected revenue instead of expected net gain. For example, a lottery ticket paying $10 with probability 0.1 has expected net gain $(10-2)(0.1)+(-2)(0.9)=-1$, not $10(0.1)=1$. Always define payoffs as net values before multiplying by probabilities.

Students treat the probability of the 'good' outcome as the sole basis for a decision — choosing whichever action has the higher probability of success — without weighting by the magnitude of each outcome. A 90% chance of gaining $1 is worse than a 10% chance of gaining $100; expected value exists precisely to account for both dimensions simultaneously.

When there are more than two outcomes, students sometimes let the probabilities sum to more than 1 by accidentally double-counting a scenario or forgetting a complementary event. Always verify that all probabilities across outcomes for a single action sum to exactly $\sum p_i = 1$ before computing $E[X]$.
untested_variants: Decision trees with sequential choices and conditional probabilities, where an initial decision changes the probability distribution over later outcomes (e.g., deciding to gather information before a second decision).
Utility-based decisions where the payoff function is nonlinear (e.g., risk-averse agents using $u(x)=\sqrt{x}$), so maximizing expected utility differs from maximizing expected monetary value.
Problems involving the Law of Total Expectation, where you compute $E[X]$ by conditioning on a partition of the sample space: $E[X] = E[X|A]P(A) + E[X|A^c]P(A^c)$.
Multi-action decision problems under uncertainty where probabilities are themselves unknown (minimax or maximin criteria instead of expected value).
